Thoughts on AI

As AI becomes more and more capable, designers need to expand their perspective on their contributions.

No longer is a purely outstanding craft knowledge enough to stand out. AI can leverage the greatest designs ever created, infinitely faster than any human.

Designers need to help discover the right problem to solve and push for the correct solution.

IF anyone can build software, the next question becomes what is worth building? Designers are in a unique position to bridge business needs and tech solutions.

We have to take it on ourselves to guide our stakeholders and project teams to drive change.
 There’s no progress without destruction.
 What can designers bring to the table when technical execution is automated?

Failures

Written on: 2025 May 14


In the quiet corners of our minds, where doubts and fears often reside, there lies a story of resilience and growth. This is a story about failures—my failures. It's a tale I've rarely shared, but in this safe space, I feel compelled to open up.

When I started considering design as a career, I had to take up and learn how to draw. Mind you back then I was not artistically inclined and everyone I was up against came from art school with years of training. I kept comparing myself to them, forever disappointed about my lack of skills. At the time the university admission was a still life drawing exercise taking 2 whole days, and a portfolio demo. I, to this day, don’t know how I got in, I felt so out of place compared to others. Objectively speaking I couldn’t see what I could bring to the table against all the other artists who attended the admission test.

During one of my semesters I failed half my courses. Half of my courses. Including a course in color. You may as well ask how it’s possible to fail a color course? Well, I can tell you. I was impatient. The task was to color perfect squares with perfectly consistent colors using water colors. I failed cause I didn’t have the patience to do it. 

I failed a sociology course while I was an exchange student. Again you’d assume exchange students get a free pass. I didn’t. I simply failed. I was too arrogant, to put enough time into studying for the exam. I thought I could wing it. I couldn’t retake the exam as my semester was up so I had to take extra courses when I got back to my own uni. 

During my uni mandated internship, I worked tirelessly on multiple projects only to get “design’s block” on multiple ones, and failed to produce any meaningful concepts worthy of showing to clients.

I almost failed a marketing class because again I was so dismissive and cynical about marketing I argued with my professor. I barely passed, but if I didn't I probably wouldn’t have enough credits by the end of my semester to be admissible for my final exam.

After graduating, I applied for hundreds of internships and jobs, and failed to land anything for about 6 months. I moved back to my parents, and was pretty hopeless. Eventually I did find an internship that I couldn’t turn into a permanent job. I thought I did everything I could, delivered what was asked but there was no opening for anything beyond my internship. All I could get is a 1 month extension. 

When I finally found a job that I was able to make stick, I was very grateful. I worked hard, but the failures kept coming. I once spent 2 weeks working on a mechanical design that just wouldn’t work. Just as I was about to give up and tell my boss I’m unable to come up with a solution, I stumbled upon a principle I could adapt and make work. It was exhilarating especially after the low I was on. 

Following this, I was working on an Operator Interface concept, and got my first experiences with UX and UI, including tools. I spent 2 months iterating on designs, not to make them better, no-no, just to make the prototype work as I initially envisioned. I made every mistake along the way including choosing the wrong tools, setting up the project the wrong way, going down dead end workflows that had to be redone almost completely. 

In a previous role, I was working with business and development teams to improve the ux of a product for a startup. Little did I know that the knowhow the company had, was purely anecdotal and had little to no actual validity. I failed to question the leadership about it, missed the point of the product and followed a path of design development that was completely unsubstantiated and wrong. Later, the company scaled back operations. 

In another project, I was working together with another designer to redesign 2 essential screens on a product that had 80-90% of the traffic. We seemingly worked closely together, split up tasks but deep down we both were frustrated with the other. After months of collaboration, we both burned out and accepted our mis-match of a solution that satisfies nothing of the original requirements. We both felt we compromised, under delivered, but at least we were “okay” with the result. Just before the project wrapped up, the other designer quit. Good riddance. The project got backlogged, but later we picked it up again. After the initial solution, I took everything I learned from those failures and redesigned the whole UX from end-to-end. Our users loved it, our own developers loved it. But we had to go through the growth pain of failing and I accepted subpar designs in the name of team harmony. 

I had to learn a lot of things the hard way, because there was no-one there to teach, mentor, or coach me. All I had was a supportive boss/manager/leadership and a problem to be solved. With every iteration I got a little better at getting things right. 

What I’m trying to get at is that I am very grateful to have been able to fail so many times. Even though I feel better about my understanding of colors now that back when I failed that course, it is still not something I’m confident in. Failing taught me humility and to always question myself. Failing helped me better ground my skills but also keeps me forever questioning the things I know. It allows me to judge things I see more objectively, but never makes me feel confident enough to my knowledge. 

In the end, this story is not just about failures but about the resilience and growth that come from them. It's about the humility to question oneself and the persistence to keep moving forward. It's about the gratitude for the lessons learned and the strength gained from each setback. And so, with every failure, I grow a little more, becoming a better version of myself.

Searching for an end-to-end work visualization solution

Over the years I continuously struggled with managing my work related todos at companies I worked for.

I started off with pen and paper, check-boxed my todos, wrote dates on the margins and indicated progress by striking through/half filling checkboxes. It worked, up until I needed to capture notes, ideas, concepts, discussions, drawings, etc to help me work. So all my todos were mixed in with my drawings, concepts, thoughts. It was impossible to find anything cause it was analog. I had limited sense of progress, and had to constantly flip back to check if I forgot something.

Eventually, shifting to digital, I used a number of tools, Gitbook, Quip, Google docs, Notion. I started using these to put my daily todos, tag them with today’s date, link to other documents, and have a running list of done tasks. Later I realized I needed a place to have future tasks or reminders, so these went at the top of the list into a pending/backlog section. For some reason, tickets never clicked with my way of thinking, so Jiras were and still are somewhat foreign to me.

As I and companies I worked for changed apps/ecosystems, I had to start from scratch every time and adapt. I got used to a lot of the apps, but it became annoying having to migrate all know-how over and over. Additionally, one big gap all these apps have, is that I could never easily visualize the types of work I was doing.

Since taking on more and more responsibilities across my career, I needed to find a way to keep my sanity and dump everything into a system that is easy to use, easy to learn, free, not company dependent, nor against company policy. Also there was no going back to analog, the benefits of digital are simply too big to give up. (I still keep sketches are some notes in my notebook, but quickly digitalize what I will need later)

So I went on to research what would tick all my boxes. After careful consideration, I actually landed on Apple’s built-in apps. They’re free, come pre-installed, efficient, and work well together. I did have to learn a few things and mentally recalibrate how to track tasks, but its wasn’t completely unfamiliar. Also these are local to the Mac without needing internet, or cloud access. Nothing gets uploaded anywhere, so it shouldn’t violate any company policy.

My biggest challenge was to move away from a daily list of todos, and group them into 3 buckets: backlog, up next, in progress. Done tasks would fall off the list.
 Let’s see how this is set up.

Central trading desk

I do have a little story for this for you to understand what the context was and how we're we are actually dealing with the problems that we are or they're facing and how we try to solve them. And here's the story. So. Yeah. So CTD, CTD stands for central trading desk.Practically, this is a person and a team working at Tower to help portfolio managers manage their portfolios and time zones that they're not available in.So that's the high level and there's an actual story I put together with Sneha. Kudos to her, even though she's not here, she helped me immensely with the discovery and research. Join sessions and organizing and keeping tabs on everything to make this sort of. Happen and the research that we've done has been very extensive and very to the point and thorough. So imagine it's Monday and you're in. You're working at central Trading desk as an employee in New York. You happen to have a portfolio manager in Japan who wants you to handle the US strategies while he's asleep. Obviously, the time some differences are pretty big. You wake up, you get about 20 to 30 instructions on what to do when the strategies. Open depending on what the market does, the markets open in an hour. You start digging through your instructions until you prioritizing. Taking notes mapping out the ifs and where whens and what ifs and trying to keep a top of everything when you're when the market actually hits and starts or starts trading. So you send those matches messages out to those strategies as quickly as possible. These messages are practically things like. Buy sell. I don't know. Stop trading platin the position. That sort of stuff. You have your notes. You have your ideas pretty much sorted out roughly, and then the market opens. Nothing spectacular happens. You start your day, you chew through your To Do List. You're 24 or 45 or whatever amount of minutes in and your 2/3 done, but then a global event happens and shakes up the market. You panic. You frantically start to revise your strategy. Try to remember which ones you sent and which ones you didn't you try to update them. You try to adjust them. You try to do anything in your power to get through the day and minimize your losses and you try to finish your list and act upon the ones that only make the only the ones that make sense.You're under stress. You're there's a lot of money at stake, and you can't really mess this up. You don't want to fail your portfolio manager. You don't want to put your job on the line. And worst of all, you don't want to disappoint your family and friends. Obviously like as I take care of what you do eventually, eventually you get through your list. You managed OK.
